verses.text: Then went Boaz up to the gate, and sat him down there: and, behold, the kinsman of whom Boaz spake came by; unto whom he said, Ho, such a one! turn aside, sit down here. And he turned aside, and sat down. He took ten men of the elders of the city, and said, Sit ye down here. And they sat down. Guidance: The city gate is the open-air courtroom. Boaz convenes a legal session before breakfast — same morning as the threshing-floor scene. 'Such a one' is the Hebrew peloni almoni — an idiom meaning 'so-and-so,' a placeholder. The narrator keeps the nearer kinsman anonymous; he will leave the story without his name carved in scripture. Ten elders are the local minyan needed to make a transaction official. Boaz is moving fast and lawful. The chapter's tone is what every prayer 'rest in the LORD' should look like: efficient action carried out openly.

Then went Boaz up to the gate, and sat him down there: and, behold, the kinsman of whom Boaz spake came by; unto whom he said, Ho, such a one! turn aside, sit down here. And he turned aside, and sat down. He took ten men of the elders of the city, and said, Sit ye down here. And they sat down. Guidance: The city gate is the open-air courtroom. Boaz convenes a legal session before breakfast — same morning as the threshing-floor scene. 'Such a one' is the Hebrew peloni almoni — an idiom meaning 'so-and-so,' a placeholder. The narrator keeps the nearer kinsman anonymous; he will leave the story without his name carved in scripture. Ten elders are the local minyan needed to make a transaction official. Boaz is moving fast and lawful. The chapter's tone is what every prayer 'rest in the LORD' should look like: efficient action carried out openly.

He said unto the kinsman, Naomi, that is come again out of the country of Moab, selleth a parcel of land, which was our brother Elimelech's: and I thought to advertise thee, saying, Buy it before the inhabitants, and before the elders of my people. If thou wilt redeem it, redeem it: but if thou wilt not redeem it, then tell me, that I may know: for there is none to redeem it beside thee; and I am after thee. And he said, I will redeem it. Guidance: Boaz frames the offer first as land alone. Elimelech's parcel is for sale; you have first right; will you buy? The nearer kinsman, hearing about real estate, immediately agrees. Land seems like a pure financial win — buy now, keep the field in the clan, profit later. The 'I will redeem it' comes too quickly; he has not asked the cost. Boaz's pause was strategic: he let the man hear the bargain before he heard the bride.

He said unto the kinsman, Naomi, that is come again out of the country of Moab, selleth a parcel of land, which was our brother Elimelech's: and I thought to advertise thee, saying, Buy it before the inhabitants, and before the elders of my people. If thou wilt redeem it, redeem it: but if thou wilt not redeem it, then tell me, that I may know: for there is none to redeem it beside thee; and I am after thee. And he said, I will redeem it. Guidance: Boaz frames the offer first as land alone. Elimelech's parcel is for sale; you have first right; will you buy? The nearer kinsman, hearing about real estate, immediately agrees. Land seems like a pure financial win — buy now, keep the field in the clan, profit later. The 'I will redeem it' comes too quickly; he has not asked the cost. Boaz's pause was strategic: he let the man hear the bargain before he heard the bride.

Then said Boaz, What day thou buyest the field of the hand of Naomi, thou must buy it also of Ruth the Moabitess, the wife of the dead, to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance. The kinsman said, I cannot redeem it for myself, lest I mar mine own inheritance: redeem thou my right to thyself; for I cannot redeem it. Guidance: Now the second clause: the land comes with Ruth. By taking her as wife and raising a son in Mahlon's name, the firstborn would inherit the parcel — meaning the kinsman would pay the cost and the property would eventually leave his family. 'Mar mine own inheritance' is the giveaway. He wanted the field; he did not want the foreign widow. He wanted asset; he did not want obligation. Money he could afford; the long-term shape of his family he could not. Both kinsmen could see the same legal package; their characters differ on which side they care about.

The kinsman said, I cannot redeem it for myself, lest I mar mine own inheritance: redeem thou my right to thyself; for I cannot redeem it. Guidance: Now the second clause: the land comes with Ruth. By taking her as wife and raising a son in Mahlon's name, the firstborn would inherit the parcel — meaning the kinsman would pay the cost and the property would eventually leave his family. 'Mar mine own inheritance' is the giveaway. He wanted the field; he did not want the foreign widow. He wanted asset; he did not want obligation. Money he could afford; the long-term shape of his family he could not. Both kinsmen could see the same legal package; their characters differ on which side they care about.

Now this was the manner in former time in Israel concerning redeeming and concerning changing, for to confirm all things; a man plucked off his shoe, and gave it to his neighbour: and this was a testimony in Israel. Therefore the kinsman said unto Boaz, Buy it for thee. So he drew off his shoe. Guidance: The narrator pauses to explain a custom obsolete by his own day — the shoe-exchange that ratified a transfer of right. The shoe was a personal article; passing it meant 'this right is yours now.' The fuller form was in Deut 25:9 (a brother-in-law's shame ritual), but this is the gentler legal version. The nearer kinsman walks home one shoe lighter and his name absent from scripture. Boaz walks home with a field, a wife, and the right to redeem. Some legal hands are larger than others.

Now this was the manner in former time in Israel concerning redeeming and concerning changing, for to confirm all things; a man plucked off his shoe, and gave it to his neighbour: and this was a testimony in Israel. Therefore the kinsman said unto Boaz, Buy it for thee. So he drew off his shoe. Guidance: The narrator pauses to explain a custom obsolete by his own day — the shoe-exchange that ratified a transfer of right. The shoe was a personal article; passing it meant 'this right is yours now.' The fuller form was in Deut 25:9 (a brother-in-law's shame ritual), but this is the gentler legal version. The nearer kinsman walks home one shoe lighter and his name absent from scripture. Boaz walks home with a field, a wife, and the right to redeem. Some legal hands are larger than others.

Boaz said unto the elders, and unto all the people, Ye are witnesses this day, that I have bought all that was Elimelech's, and all that was Chilion's and Mahlon's, of the hand of Naomi. Moreover Ruth the Moabitess, the wife of Mahlon, have I purchased to be my wife, to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance, that the name of the dead be not cut off from among his brethren, and from the gate of his place: ye are witnesses this day. Guidance: Boaz's formal declaration. He names Elimelech, Chilion, and Mahlon — the three dead men of chapter 1 — and announces he is bringing their names back from oblivion. The narrator's emphasis: 'that the name of the dead be not cut off.' Ruth's first husband Mahlon will have a legal son through Boaz; the family line that was extinguished in Moab is being grafted back at Bethlehem. Boaz's marriage is partly love, partly law, partly mercy to dead brothers. The fullest hesed in scripture is care extended to people who can never repay you, even people no longer alive to feel it.

Boaz said unto the elders, and unto all the people, Ye are witnesses this day, that I have bought all that was Elimelech's, and all that was Chilion's and Mahlon's, of the hand of Naomi. Moreover Ruth the Moabitess, the wife of Mahlon, have I purchased to be my wife, to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance, that the name of the dead be not cut off from among his brethren, and from the gate of his place: ye are witnesses this day. Guidance: Boaz's formal declaration. He names Elimelech, Chilion, and Mahlon — the three dead men of chapter 1 — and announces he is bringing their names back from oblivion. The narrator's emphasis: 'that the name of the dead be not cut off.' Ruth's first husband Mahlon will have a legal son through Boaz; the family line that was extinguished in Moab is being grafted back at Bethlehem. Boaz's marriage is partly love, partly law, partly mercy to dead brothers. The fullest hesed in scripture is care extended to people who can never repay you, even people no longer alive to feel it.

All the people that were in the gate, and the elders, said, We are witnesses. The LORD make the woman that is come into thine house like Rachel and like Leah, which two did build the house of Israel: and do thou worthily in Ephratah, and be famous in Beth-lehem: and let thy house be like the house of Pharez, whom Tamar bare unto Judah, of the seed which the LORD shall give thee of this young woman. Guidance: The town blesses Ruth by comparing her to Rachel and Leah — the matriarchs who built Israel. A Moabite widow has just been canonized in the same list as Jacob's wives. Then a stranger comparison: Pharez (Perez), son of Tamar and Judah. Tamar's was an ugly story (Gen 38) — a widow who tricked her father-in-law into providing a son. The elders are quietly acknowledging that even unusual unions can yield faithful descendants. They are also blessing for fruitfulness in 'Ephrathah, in Bethlehem' — the same town Micah 5:2 will pinpoint as the Messiah's birthplace. The elders bless better than they know.

All the people that were in the gate, and the elders, said, We are witnesses. The LORD make the woman that is come into thine house like Rachel and like Leah, which two did build the house of Israel: and do thou worthily in Ephratah, and be famous in Beth-lehem: and let thy house be like the house of Pharez, whom Tamar bare unto Judah, of the seed which the LORD shall give thee of this young woman. So Boaz took Ruth, and she was his wife: and when he went in unto her, the LORD gave her conception, and she bare a son. Guidance: One sentence. Marriage, consummation, conception, birth. Three things the chapter does in three clauses, with the LORD as the active verb on conception. Ten years of marriage to Mahlon in Moab produced no child; one act under Boaz's roof produces one immediately. The narrator credits the LORD: He had closed the womb in Moab and opens it in Bethlehem. The longest period of Ruth's life took its shape from a different city than its closing scene. Sometimes faith is the patience to find out that geography mattered less than the LORD's calendar.

The women said unto Naomi, Blessed be the LORD, which hath not left thee this day without a kinsman, that his name may be famous in Israel. And he shall be unto thee a restorer of thy life, and a nourisher of thine old age: for thy daughter in law, which loveth thee, which is better to thee than seven sons, hath born him. Guidance: The town women, who had asked 'Is this Naomi?' on a bitter day in chapter 1, now bless the LORD over Naomi. They use the same word, kinsman (goel), Naomi taught Ruth in chapter 2. Two phrases stand out: 'restorer of thy life' (the same root as the Hebrew word for repentance — turning back from death), and 'a nourisher of thine old age' (a verb of sustenance, the thing widows feared most). Then the chapter's most generous compliment: Ruth is 'better to thee than seven sons.' In a society that prized sons above all, a Moabite daughter-in-law has been weighed and found to outweigh seven sons. The book that began naming Mahlon and Chilion ('sickly' and 'failing') ends praising a woman as worth more than a complete brood.

The women said unto Naomi, Blessed be the LORD, which hath not left thee this day without a kinsman, that his name may be famous in Israel. Naomi took the child, and laid it in her bosom, and became nurse unto it. The women her neighbours gave it a name, saying, There is a son born to Naomi; and they called his name Obed: he is the father of Jesse, the father of David. Guidance: Naomi cradles the baby in her bosom. The verb is the same one used of the LORD carrying His people. The empty grandmother now has a grandson to nurse. The neighborhood names the child 'Obed' (meaning 'servant' or 'worshiper'). And then the book's secret reveal: 'he is the father of Jesse, the father of David.' For four chapters the narrator has waited to drop the name. The whole story has been the slow biography of King David's great-grandmother. A Moabite widow's loyalty becomes the line of the Messiah. Matthew 1:5 will quote this verse as part of the gospel genealogy. Every act of hesed in the book has been quietly building the family tree of Jesus.

Now these are the generations of Pharez: Pharez begat Hezron, and Hezron begat Ram, and Ram begat Amminadab, and Amminadab begat Nahshon, and Nahshon begat Salmon, and Salmon begat Boaz, and Boaz begat Obed, and Obed begat Jesse, and Jesse begat David. Guidance: The book ends with a ten-name genealogy from Pharez to David — exactly the length of the great patriarch lists in Genesis. The book began in the days of the judges (era of chaos) and closes pointing forward to a king (era of David). Notice some names: Nahshon (Aaron's brother-in-law, Num 1:7), Salmon (according to Matthew 1:5, married Rahab the Canaanite of Jericho). Salmon-Rahab-Boaz; Boaz-Ruth-Obed. Two Gentile women in two consecutive generations. The line of David — and thus of Christ — is half-Moabite and half-Canaanite by great-grandmothers. The book of Ruth is the LORD's quiet announcement that the gospel was always going to include the foreigner. Behind a barren widow's lament, behind a midnight on a threshing floor, behind a shoe traded at a gate, the King of kings is being arranged. Sometimes the LORD writes salvation in the small print of one widow's loyalty. The book closes with a single name held up high: David. The reader knows the next book begins immediately, and somewhere past it, Bethlehem will again become a name to remember.
